{{Short description|Practise of standing in a powerful way}}
{{medref|date=September 2023}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=October 2024}}
[[File:Power pose by Amy Cuddy at PopTech 2011 (6279920726).jpg|thumb| Amy Cuddy demonstrating her theory of "power posing" with a photo of the comic-book superhero [[Wonder Woman]]]]
'''Power posing''' is the practice of standing in a posture mentally associated with being powerful, in the hope of feeling more confident and behaving more assertively. Though the underlying science is disputed, its promoters argue that people can foster positive life changes simply by assuming a "powerful" or "expansive" [[Posture (psychology)|posture]] for a few minutes before an interaction in which confidence is needed. One popular image of the technique in practice is that of candidates "lock[ing] themselves in bathroom stalls before job interviews to make victory V's with their arms."<ref name="NYT_2017_Cuddy" />

Power posing was first suggested in a 2010 paper by [[Dana R. Carney]], [[Amy Cuddy]], and [[Andy Yap]] in the journal ''[[Psychological Science]]'',<ref name="CarneyCuddyYap2010"/> and came to prominence through a popular TED talk by Cuddy in 2012.<ref name="CuddyTED">{{cite web |last1=Cuddy |first1=Amy |title=Your body language may shape who you are |url=https://www.ted.com/talks/amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_who_you_are?language=en |website=[[TED (conference)|TED]] |access-date=6 July 2019 |language=en |date=June 2012}}</ref> However, in 2015 several researchers began reporting that the effect could not be replicated,<ref name="Ranehill_2015" /><ref name="Simonsohn_Simmons_2016" /><ref>{{cite web |last1=King |first1=Tracy |title=Sajid Javid and the strange science behind power poses |url=https://www.theguardian.com/science/head-quarters/2018/may/01/sajid-javid-and-the-strange-science-behind-power-poses |website=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=19 June 2018 |language=en |date=1 May 2018}}</ref> and, in 2016, Carney issued a statement abandoning the theory.<ref name="Carney_nd_Myposition" /> Cuddy, however, continued her research,<ref name="Singal Dahl Amy Cuddy's Response" /><ref name="NYT_2017_Cuddy" /> claiming to have evidence that posture feedback can at least make people ''feel'' more powerful.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Cuddy|first1=Amy J. C.|last2=Schultz|first2=S. Jack|last3=Fosse|first3=Nathan E.|date=2 March 2018|title=P-Curving a More Comprehensive Body of Research on Postural Feedback Reveals Clear Evidential Value for Power-Posing Effects: Reply to Simmons and Simonsohn (2017)|journal=[[Psychological Science]]|volume=29|issue=4|pages=656–666|doi=10.1177/0956797617746749|pmid=29498906|s2cid=3675226}}</ref><ref name="Elsesser2018">{{Cite news|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/kimelsesser/2018/04/03/power-posing-is-back-amy-cuddy-successfully-refutes-criticism/#3e8fd3383b8e|title=Power Posing Is Back: Amy Cuddy Successfully Refutes Criticism|last=Elsesser|first=Kim|work=[[Forbes]]|date=3 April 2018|access-date=2018-07-17|language=en}}</ref><ref name="BPS2018">{{cite web |last1=Young |first1=Emma |title=54-study analysis says power posing does affect people's emotions and is worth researching further |url=https://digest.bps.org.uk/2018/03/28/54-study-analysis-says-power-posing-does-affect-peoples-emotions-and-is-worth-researching-further/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180523001945/https://digest.bps.org.uk/2018/03/28/54-study-analysis-says-power-posing-does-affect-peoples-emotions-and-is-worth-researching-further/ |archive-date=23 May 2018 |website=British Psychological Society Research Digest |date=28 March 2018 |access-date=14 July 2019}}</ref> Today, power posing is often cited as an example of the [[replication crisis]] in the sciences.<ref name="Aschwanden 2016">{{cite web|last1=Aschwanden|first1=Christine|title=Failure is moving science forward|website=[[FiveThirtyEight]]|date= March 24, 2016 |url=https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/failure-is-moving-science-forward/}}</ref>

== Initial claims ==
The initial research on power posing was published in 2010. Dana Carney, Amy Cuddy, and Andy Yap claimed that high-power poses "produce power". The study included 42 participants, who were coached by researchers to assume a physical position of power. Hormone levels were measured before and after, and the authors stated that they found an increase in [[testosterone]] and a decrease in [[cortisol]] after posing.<ref name="NYT_2017_Cuddy"/> The researchers themselves suggested a range of possible real-world applications:

{{quote|These findings suggest that, in some situations requiring power, people have the ability to "[[Fake it till you make it|fake it 'til they make it]]." Over time and in aggregate, these minimal postural changes and their outcomes potentially could improve a person's general health and well-being. This potential benefit is particularly important when considering people who are or who feel chronically powerless because of lack of resources, low hierarchical rank in an organization, or membership in a low-power social group."<ref name="CarneyCuddyYap2010">{{cite journal |title=Power Posing: Brief Nonverbal Displays Affect Neuroendocrine Levels and Risk Tolerance |first1=Dana R. |last1=Carney |first2=Amy J.C. |last2=Cuddy |first3=Andy J. |last3=Yap |url=https://www0.gsb.columbia.edu/mygsb/faculty/research/pubfiles/4679/power.poses_.PS_.2010.pdf |journal=[[Psychological Science]] |volume=21 |number=10 |pages=1363–1368|doi=10.1177/0956797610383437 |pmid=20855902 |date=January 10, 2010|s2cid=1126623 }}</ref>}}

The researchers concluded that power posing induces lasting hormonal changes, which can lead to better outcomes in work-related situations, such as job interviews and wage negotiations.

== Replication failures and meta-analyses ==
The earliest criticism of Carney, Cuddy and Yap's work came from Steven Stanton, who noted their lack of attention to gender differences. "Carney et al. used a novel manipulation to ask an important question," he concluded, "but the degree to which their findings can be fully understood and implemented into future research is questionable without more complete analyses."<ref name="Stanton2011">{{cite journal|title=The Essential Implications of Gender in Human Behavioral Endocrinology Studies|first=Steven J.|last=Stanton|year=2011|journal=[[Frontiers in Behavioral Neuroscience]]|volume=5|page=9|doi=10.3389/fnbeh.2011.00009|pmid = 21441984|pmc=3057631|doi-access=free }}</ref>

A 2015 article, published in ''Psychological Science'' by Ranehill et al. reported the results of a conceptual replication of the study using a larger sample. The researchers confirmed Carney et al.'s results about ''felt'' power but could not detect any physiological or behavioral effects of power posing.<ref name="Ranehill_2015">{{cite journal |last1=Ranehill |first1=Eva |last2=Dreber |first2=Anna |last3=Johannesson |first3=Magnus |last4=Leiberg |first4=Susanne |last5=Sul |first5=Sunhae |last6=Weber |first6=Roberto A. |date=May 26, 2015 |title=Assessing the Robustness of Power Posing: No Effect on Hormones and Risk Tolerance in a Large Sample of Men and Women |journal=Psychological Science |pmid=25810452 |doi=10.1177/0956797614553946 |volume=26 |number=5 |pages=653–6 |s2cid=28372856 |url=http://datacolada.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/5110-Ranehill-Dreber-Johannesson-Leiberg-Sul-Weber-PS-2015-Assessing-the-robustness-of-power-posing-no-effect-on-hormones-and-risk-rolerance-in-a-large-sample-of-men-and-women.pdf}}</ref> The statistical methods that may have led to the original erroneous findings were reviewed by [[Uri Simonsohn]] and Joseph Simmons of the [[Wharton School]] in a 2016 paper, concluding that the current body of research fails to "suggest the existence of an effect once we account for [[selective reporting]]".<ref name="Simonsohn_Simmons_2016">{{cite journal |title=Power Posing: P-Curving the Evidence |series=Psychological Science (forthcoming) |date=June 10, 2016 |first1=Joseph P. |last1=Simmons |journal=Data Colada |ssrn=2791272 }}</ref><ref name="Another failed replication">{{cite web|last1=Gelman|first1=Andrew|author-link1=Andrew Gelman|title=Another failed replication of power pose|url=http://andrewgelman.com/2016/10/22/30294/|website=Statistical Modeling, Causal Inference, and Social Science|date=22 October 2016|access-date=November 6, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Beyond power pose">{{cite web|last1=Gelman|first1=Andrew|author-link1=Andrew Gelman|title= Beyond "power pose": Using replication failures and a better understanding of data collection and analysis to do better science |url=http://andrewgelman.com/2017/10/18/beyond-power-pose-using-replication-failures-better-understanding-data-collection-analysis-better-science/|website=Statistical Modeling, Causal Inference, and Social Science|date=18 October 2017|access-date=November 6, 2017}}</ref>

In the years that followed, attempts were made by various research groups to apply power posing manipulation in different contexts. The results did not support the assumptions made by Cuddy et al. In a 2016 study by Garrison et al. the effect of posture manipulation was combined with dominant vs. submissive gaze. However, no effect was found on risk taking and, in contrast to original expectations, adopting an expansive pose reduced feelings of power.<ref>{{Cite journal | doi=10.1177/1948550616652209|title = Embodying Power| journal=[[Social Psychological and Personality Science]]| volume=7| issue=7| pages=623–630|year = 2016|last1 = Garrison|first1 = Katie E.| last2=Tang| first2=David| last3=Schmeichel| first3=Brandon J.|s2cid = 53520700}}</ref> Deuter et al. (2016) investigated the effect of cognitive role taking and Cuddy's power posing manipulation in the [[Trier Social Stress Test]]; although role taking had an influence on the cortisol and testosterone response after stress, the posture manipulation had no effect on hormonal, behavioral or subjective measures.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Deuter |first1=Christian Eric|last2=Schächinger|first2=Hartmut|last3=Best|first3=Daniel|last4=Neumann|first4=Roland|date=September 2016 |title=Effects of two dominance manipulations on the stress response: Cognitive and embodied influences |journal=[[Biological Psychology (journal)|Biological Psychology]] |pmid=27381928|doi=10.1016/j.biopsycho.2016.06.004 |volume=119|pages=184–189 |s2cid=25385820}}</ref>

In a study conducted by Smith et al. in 2017, participants had to compete in a challenging task while they had to assume high or low power poses. The authors report no main effect of pose type on testosterone, cortisol, risk or feelings of power. However, they found an interaction between pose type and competition outcome on testosterone: while winners assigned to a high-power pose had small increases in testosterone levels, losers had a reduction in testosterone after holding high-power poses.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Winners, losers, and posers: The effect of power poses on testosterone and risk-taking following competition|first1=Kristopher|last1=Smith|first2=Coren |last2=Apicella|year=2017|journal=[[Hormones and Behavior]]|volume=92|pages=172–181|pmid=27840104|doi=10.1016/j.yhbeh.2016.11.003|s2cid=205807915}}</ref>

In 2016, Dana Carney, who had been the lead author on the original 2010 paper and had supported the publication of the 2015 Ranehill et al. replication attempt, published a statement on the University of California, Berkeley website, stating that she no longer believed the effect was valid: "I do not believe that 'power pose effects' are real...the evidence against the existence of power poses is undeniable."<ref name="NYT_2017_Cuddy">{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/18/magazine/when-the-revolution-came-for-amy-cuddy.html |title=When the Revolution Came for Amy Cuddy |first1=Susan |last1=Dominus |date=October 18, 2017 |work=[[New York Times Magazine]]}}</ref><ref name=Carney_nd_Myposition>{{cite web |url=http://faculty.haas.berkeley.edu/dana_carney/pdf_my%20position%20on%20power%20poses.pdf |title=My position on "Power Poses" |first=Dana R. |last=Carney |access-date=October 19, 2017 |date=nd}}</ref><ref name="Singal co-author">{{cite web|last1=Singal|first1=Jesse|title='Power Posing' Co-author: 'I Do Not Believe That 'Power Pose' Effects Are Real'|url=http://nymag.com/scienceofus/2016/09/power-poses-co-author-i-dont-think-power-poses-are-real.html|website=[[New York Magazine]]|date=26 September 2016 |access-date=October 21, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Singal Dahl Amy Cuddy's Response">{{cite web|last1=Singal|first1=Jesse|last2=Dahl|first2=Melissa|title=Here Is Amy Cuddy's Response to Critiques of Her Power-Posing Research|url=http://nymag.com/scienceofus/2016/09/read-amy-cuddys-response-to-power-posing-critiques.html|website=[[New York Magazine]]|date=30 September 2016 |access-date=October 21, 2017}}</ref>

Joseph Cesario, an associate professor of psychology at [[Michigan State University]], who co-edits ''Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology'', arranged a special issue on power posing that published in June 2017; the issue included eleven new studies, along with a meta-analyses, which found that the effect of power posing on power behaviors was not replicated.<ref name="Simonsohn_Simmons_2016"/><ref name="Fortune_2016">{{cite web |url=http://fortune.com/2016/10/02/power-poses-research-false/ |title='Power Poses' Researcher Dana Carney Now Says Effects are "Undeniably" False |work=[[Fortune (magazine)|Fortune]] |date=October 2, 2016  |first1=David Z. |last1=Morris }}</ref><ref name="Time_2017">{{cite magazine |title='Power Poses' Don't Actually Work. Try These Confidence-Boosting Strategies Instead |first1=Jeffrey |last1=Kluger |date=September 26, 2017 |access-date=October 19, 2017 |url=https://time.com/4949675/power-poses-confidence/ |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]]}}</ref><ref name="Cesario_Henion_2017">{{cite web |url=http://datacolada.org/37 |date=September 10, 2017 |access-date=October 19, 2017 |title=Eleven New Studies Suggest 'Power Poses' Don't Work |website=DataColada |first1=Joseph |last1=Cesario |first2=Andy |last2=Henion}}</ref><ref name="newsweek_2017">{{cite web |title='Power poses' don't really make you more powerful, nine more studies confirm |first1=Meghan |last1=Bartels |date=September 13, 2017 |url=http://www.newsweek.com/power-poses-dont-make-you-more-powerful-studies-664261 |work=[[Newsweek]] |access-date=October 19, 2017}}</ref> The published studies were designed to answer whether the power-posing hypothesis was real and included high quality research features like [[Replication crisis#Pre-registration of studies|pre-registration]] of endpoints.<ref name=Carney2017intro/> Carney co-authored the introduction to the issue, and noted that while the meta-analysis failed to find any effect in power behaviors, it did find a medium-sized effect in a feeling of power; she also wrote that the studies could not resolve whether the effect on a feeling of power was only an experimental artifact.<ref name=Carney2017intro>{{cite journal|last1=Cesario|first1=Joseph|last2=Jonas|first2=Kai J.|last3=Carney|first3=Dana R.|title=CRSP special issue on power poses: what was the point and what did we learn?|journal=Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology|date=June 28, 2017|volume=2|issue=1|pages=1–5|doi=10.1080/23743603.2017.1309876|doi-access=free}}</ref>

In 2017, a meta-study by Cuddy et.al, surveyed 55 studies about power poses, and found "strong evidential value for postural-feedback (i.e., power-posing) effects and particularly robust evidential value for effects on emotional and affective states (e.g., mood and evaluations, attitudes, and feelings about the self)"<ref>{{Cite journal | doi=10.1177/0956797617746749|title = P-Curving a More Comprehensive Body of Research on Postural Feedback Reveals Clear Evidential Value for Power-Posing Effects: Reply to Simmons and Simonsohn| journal=[[Psychological Science]]| year = 2018|last1 = Cuddy|first1 = Amy| last2=Schultz | first2=Jack | last3=Fosse| first3=Nathan |volume=29| issue=4| pages=656–666| pmid=29498906 | s2cid=3675226 }}</ref>

A comprehensive meta-analytic review that analyzed 128 studies on the topic of body postures such as power posing, considering both published and unpublished papers, suggests that power posing has a reliable effect on thoughts and feelings (e.g., positive mood, self-esteem, feelings of dominance). However, power posing has no effect on physiological measures (e.g., hormone levels, blood pressure, skin conductance). Although the authors report an effect on behavioral measures, it remains unclear whether this effect actually exists or is due to selective reporting of significant results. In addition, the researchers point to limitations of the power posing literature: Few studies have included a control group (neutral posture), so it remains unclear whether the effect comes from dominant postures (so-called high power poses) or from submissive, slumped postures (so-called low power poses).<ref name=PsychBul>{{cite journal |last1=Körner |first1=Robert |last2=Röseler |first2=Lukas |last3=Schütz |first3=Astrid |last4=Bushman |first4=Brad J. |title=Dominance and prestige: Meta-analytic review of experimentally induced body position effects on behavioral, self-report, and physiological dependent variables. |journal=[[Psychological Bulletin]] |date=January 2022 |volume=148 |issue=1–2 |pages=67–85 |doi=10.1037/bul0000356}}</ref>

== Confounded tests of power posing ==
{{Technical|section|date=October 2024}}
Several researchers noted the lack of control groups in many power posing studies. Many studies had only compared power poses to contractive poses like slouching but had failed to include a normal pose as a control group.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Crede |first1=Marcus |title=A Negative Effect of a Contractive Pose is not Evidence for the Positive Effect of an Expansive Pose: Comment on Cuddy, Schultz, and Fosse (2018) |journal=Meta-Psychology |date=2019 |volume=3 |doi=10.15626/MP.2019.1723 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=PsychBul/>

The problem falls under a general problem called the "poison-medicine" problem;<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Lonati|first1=Sirio|last2=Quiroga|first2=Bernardo F.|last3=Zehnder|first3=Christian|last4=Antonakis|first4=John|date=2018|title=On doing relevant and rigorous experiments: Review and recommendations|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1016/j.jom.2018.10.003|journal=Journal of Operations Management|language=en|volume=64|issue=1|pages=19–40|doi=10.1016/j.jom.2018.10.003|s2cid=159436924 |issn=1873-1317}}</ref> comparing a medicine (m) to a poison (p) would not establish if the medicine works in promoting longevity (y) if a baseline (neutral, i.e. placebo) condition is not included because if y(m) > y(p) this difference could arise for a multitude of reasons including that (a) m does not work and p reduces y, or (b) m reduced y but p reduces y more. That a difference is observed in y(m) and y(p) does not necessarily mean that it was caused by the expected treatment effect. This issue is often overlooked in testing some psychology theories wherein incorrect comparisons have been made (e.g., in [[Adam Galinsky|Galinsky]]-type power priming studies, where a high and low power prime are often compared;<ref name=":0" /> when a baseline is included, priming of this sort creates an asymmetric demand effect, which precludes making correct causal inference).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Khademi |first1=M. |date=2021-02-01 |title=The problem of demand effects in power studies: Moving beyond power priming |journal=The Leadership Quarterly |language=en |volume=32 |issue=4 |article-number=101496 |doi=10.1016/j.leaqua.2021.101496 |issn=1048-9843 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sturm |first1=Rachel E. |last2=Antonakis |first2=John |date=2015-01-01 |title=Interpersonal Power: A Review, Critique, and Research Agenda |journal=Journal of Management |language=en |volume=41 |issue=1 |pages=136–163 |doi=10.1177/0149206314555769 |issn=0149-2063 |s2cid=73651701}}</ref>

==Public attention==
Since its promotion in a 2010 [[Harvard Business School]] ''Working Knowledge'' post,<ref name="CuddyWorkingKnow">{{cite web |last1=Hanna |first1=Julia |title=Power Posing: Fake It Until You Make It |url=https://hbswk.hbs.edu/item/power-posing-fake-it-until-you-make-it |website=[[Harvard Business School]] Working Knowledge|date=20 September 2010 }}</ref> [[Amy Cuddy]] has been the most visible proponent of power posing in the public sphere. Her interest in "studying how people can become their aspirational selves" stems from her own experience of recovering from head trauma after a car accident.<ref name="NYT_2017_Cuddy" /> The power posing [[life hack|"hack"]] gained wide attention after a [[TED talk]] she gave in 2012, where she demonstrated the posture and argued for its benefits.<ref name="CuddyTED" /> The technique was then covered by [[CNN]] and [[Oprah Winfrey]]; it was the centerpiece of her 2015 book ''Presence: Bringing your boldest self to your biggest challenges''; and by 2017 her TED talk had been viewed by about 47 million viewers, becoming the second most popular.<ref name="NYT_2017_Cuddy" />

In 2015, several news outlets in the [[United Kingdom]] said that some members of the [[Conservative Party (UK)|UK Conservative Party]] had begun to adopt a "bizarre" wide stance at high-profile political events, which some suggested was based on Cuddy's 'power posing' advice.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/conservative-weird-legs-apart/|title=A body language expert on why Sajid Javid (and other Conservatives) use the bizarre 'power pose'|date=2018-04-30|work=[[i (British newspaper)|iNews]]|access-date=2018-04-30|language=en-GB|first1=Ruchira|last1=Sharma}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/sajid-javid-adopts-awkward-tory-12453896|title=New Home Secretary Sajid Javid adopts awkward Tory 'wide standing' stance|last=Smith|first=Mikey|date=2018-04-30|work=[[Daily Mirror]]|access-date=2018-04-30}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.indy100.com/article/tories-keep-doing-that-incredibly-weird-thing-with-their-legs--bk91vaHsXZ|title=Tories keep doing that incredibly weird thing with their legs|date=2016-06-03|work=[[The Independent|indy100]]|access-date=2018-04-30|first1=Evan|last1=Bartlett}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-43960452|title=When is it OK to do a 'power pose'?|last1=Wheeler|first1=Brian|date=2018-05-01|work=[[BBC News]]|access-date=2018-05-01}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2018/apr/30/sajid-javid-tory-power-stance|title=Sajid Javid and the return of the Tory power stance|first1=Martin|last1=Belam|date=2018-04-30|newspaper=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=2019-07-06}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/tory-power-stance-ridiculed-by-speaking-coach-as-pose-of-a-right-plonker-sc8098kr5|title=Tory power stance ridiculed by speaking coach as pose of a right plonker|first1=Grant|last1=Tucker|newspaper=[[The Times]]|date=2018-05-05|access-date=2019-07-06}}</ref> While this was referred to by some as the "Tory power pose",<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/blogs-the-papers-43957420|title=Newspaper headlines: Javid's 'fresh start' and UK faces care crisis|date=2018-05-01|work=[[BBC News]]|access-date=2018-05-01}}</ref><ref name="Khaleeli">{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/shortcuts/2015/oct/07/who-told|title=A body language lesson gone wrong: why is George Osborne standing like Beyoncé?|last=Khaleeli|first=Homa|date=2015-10-07|work=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=2018-04-30}}</ref> it had previously been used by [[Labour Party (UK)|Labour]] [[Prime Minister]] [[Tony Blair]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://i-d.co/article/whats-with-the-weird-power-stance-people-keep-doing/|work=[[i-D]]|title=what's with the weird power stance people keep doing?|first1=Hanna|last1=Hanra|date=3 May 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221007052226/https://i-d.vice.com/en/article/ne9aqq/whats-with-the-weird-power-stance-people-keep-doing|archive-date=7 October 2022|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/conservative-weird-legs-apart/|title=A body language expert on why Sajid Javid (and other Conservatives) use the bizarre 'power pose'|date=2018-04-30|work=[[i (newspaper)|iNews]]|access-date=2018-04-30|first1=Ruchira|last1=Sharma}}</ref><ref name="Khaleeli"/> Politicians publicly photographed in this stance include [[Sajid Javid]], [[George Osborne]], [[David Cameron]],<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/uk-news/david-cameron-caught-awkward-tory-8105678|title=Has David Cameron finally caught the Tory 'wide standing' disease?|last1=Smith|first1=Mikey|date=2016-06-02|work=[[Daily Mirror]]|access-date=2018-04-30}}</ref> [[Tony Blair]],<ref name="metro">{{cite news|url=https://metro.co.uk/2018/04/30/tories-keep-standing-like-7510305|work=[[Metro (British newspaper)|Metro]]|title=Why do Tories keep standing like this?|first1=Jen|last1=Mills|date=30 April 2018}}</ref> and [[Theresa May]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2018/apr/30/sajid-javid-tory-power-stance|title=Sajid Javid and the return of the Tory power stance|last1=Belam|first1=Martin|date=2018-04-30|work=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=2018-04-30}}</ref><ref name="metro" />

By 2016, public discussion of power posing had shifted to the difficulty of replicating the effect in subsequent studies. An extensive series of articles on power posing replication was published by [[New York (magazine)|''New York'' magazine]] by [[Jesse Singal]] and other contributors in its ''Science of Us'' section.<ref name="Singal Dahl Amy Cuddy's Response" /><ref name="Singal co-author" /><ref name="Interesting House-of-Cards Element to the Fall of Power Poses">{{cite web|last1=Singal|first1=Jesse|title=There's an Interesting House-of-Cards Element to the Fall of Power Poses|url=http://nymag.com/scienceofus/2016/09/the-fall-of-power-poses-has-a-house-of-cards-aspect-to-it.html|website=[[New York Magazine]]|date=27 September 2016 |access-date=October 21, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Why People Love(d) Power Posing: A Science of Us Conversation">{{cite web|last1=Romm|first1=Cari|last2=Baer|first2=Drake|last3=Singal|first3=Jesse|last4=Dahl|first4=Melissa|title=Why People Love(d) Power Posing: A Science of Us Conversation|url=http://nymag.com/scienceofus/2016/09/what-to-do-now-if-power-poses-used-to-help-you.html|website=[[New York Magazine]]|date=30 September 2016 |access-date=October 21, 2017}}</ref> There was intense controversy around these issues and Cuddy reported experiencing harassment, including death threats, after the findings were not replicated.<ref name="How Should We Talk About Amy Cuddy, Death Threats, and the Replication Crisis?">{{cite web|last1=Singal|first1=Jesse|title=How Should We Talk About Amy Cuddy, Death Threats, and the Replication Crisis?|url=http://nymag.com/scienceofus/2017/04/amy-cuddy-death-threats.html|website=[[New York Magazine]]|date=25 April 2017 |access-date=October 21, 2017}}</ref> In the spring of 2017, Cuddy left Harvard but continues to promote power posing as life-improvement technique.<ref name="NYT_2017_Cuddy" /><ref name="Elsesser2018"/>
